content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. People who are desensitized to horror movies, what’s a movie that actually scared you?

**Score:** 191
**Body:** I love horror movies and enjoy them. It just sucks that when you’re so desensitized, nothing really scared you.

The last two movies that scared me with the lights off watching them by myself was Lights Out the first time I saw it (only because it reminded me of my childhood fear of the dark) and The Poughkeepsie Tapes because it was so disturbing. I’ve seen a lot of great horror movies since then but nothing that’s done more than startled me. What’s one that’s really scared you? I have not seen Barbarian. Some have said that is. Weapons wasn’t scary at all. Neither was Longlegs. Same with The Innkeepers. Same with Talk to Me. I liked them all, but they didn’t even move the needle.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The mothman starring Richard Gere. I was surprised that could still actually scare me. 

I'm a little desensitized too but I don't mind it. I like horror for more than just the scare. I like the tension and the suspense and t
he feeling that almost anything can go wrong. I like the feeling of danger, which is slightly different than actual jumpscare stuff.
  - Mothman Prophecies btw for anyone trying to look it up
2. For me it’s always about what you don’t see, or situations that evoke real world fears (claustrophobia, thalassophobia etc) that freaks me out. So pure gore, jump scares or a VFX unit going batshit on screen does nothing for me.

Something like the Jupiters Claim audiences fate in Nope, most of The Descent or the final scenes in The Borderlands / Wicked Prayer freak me out. 

Beyond that, I find the loft sequence in \[REC\] or any of the moments when Nicki is entirely obscured by shadow in Obsession are far more effective than some crazy assed torture porn sequence (I think Evil Dead 2’s comedic over the top blood use cured me of that)
  - The Vanishing (1988)
  - Also Nikki's unnatural and inhuman movements at times
3. The Descent is the only one that truly freaked me out
  - It's like a triple layer of horror: The deep dark unknown earth. Extreme claustrophobia. And >!The cave monsters!<
4. I think some of you misunderstood the question. If you’re frightened by The Conjuring, Bring Her Back, or even Hereditary, then you wouldn’t fall in the “desensitized to horror” category.
5. Funny Games (1997) made me so sick it made me want to stop watching horror for a little while. It’s so psychological and is actually commentary on how desensitized we are. It shows no gore as an eff you to sensationalist gore porn horror lovers and it really doesn’t need it.

As far as “scary,” Gonjiam Haunted Asylum
6. [Inequality for All](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/159008-inequality-for-all) (2013)

## 2. Being there (1979)

**Score:** 22
**Body:** In this movie Peter  Sellers  is playing  simple minded garder who ends being advisor  to some very powerful  people. Peter Sellers  was good in this movie , he was funny but very different than any other off his roles.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I always recommend reading the novella the movie is based on.  
  - I like to watch.
2. It's a very appealing, gentle film. Often quoted as the inspiration for Forrest Gump.
3. That movie was Sellers' crowning achievement, which is *really* saying something.
4. Great movie and wonderfully music scripted with parts of Erik Satie's Gnossiennes.
  - Actually the walkout at the end was not Satie. Close, but was a new piece for the film. 
5. One of my top five, forever. 
6. Masterpiece 
7. It’s one of my favorite movies, but the book is much better.
8. Been on my watchlist for ages. Might take a look.

## 3. surreal movies taking place over 24h or less

**Score:** 17
**Body:** watching Neighbors rn, others off the top of my head are Brazil and After Hours. don't have to be comedies but it seems to come with the territory?

  
edit: must all happen in the "real" world, even if it's the future. so no Existenz or Good Luck Have Fun Don't Die. can include some exposition, a la Dog Eat Dog. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Enter the Void - 2009
  - I went in not knowing anything about this film, and I was suuuuuuuperduper high. 

By the end of it I had almost puked twice, the tension in my neck and shoulders from the stress was almost unbearable, and I held my breath on and off the entire film, so felt suffocated for most of it. 

Fucking incredible film. Will never watch again. 
2. Run Lola Run
3. It’s what’s inside, Palm springs
4. Dr. Strangelove … you might call The Thing (1982) surreal.
5. Holy Motors (2012)
6. Nowhere (1997) scratched the same itch as After Hours for me, just get ready for a trip lol
7. Existenz 
8. Good Luck, Have Fun, Don't Die
9. 11:14

## 4. What are the most fun movies you have ever seen?

**Score:** 15
**Body:** I watched The Amazing Spiderman 2 today for the first time and while it wasn’t perfect in terms of story and other stuff, it was incredibly fun to watch, especially the second half.  
So i wanted some movie recommendations based off of fun factor alone, its alright if characters, story, pacing, etc is mediocre. I just want your most fun to watch movie recommendations!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Fifth Element is the most fun I think it’s possible to have watching a movie. 
2. True Lies
3. The Lego Batman Movie (2017)
4. Tremors
5. Galaxy Quest 
6. Bullet Train; non-stop chaotic action, and hilarious dialogue. The story is simple but the pacing never lets you breathe.
7. Tropic Thunder
8. **Big Trouble in Little China**

Just hit it's 40th Anniversary, this week.
9. A couple obvious ones:  Back to the Future and Indiana Jones (particularly Raiders of Lost Ark and The Last Crusade)

Ocean's Eleven (2001) - this movie, for me, is the textbook definition of fun

The Rocketeer (1991)

Midnight Run (1988)

Mission: Impossible franchise - especially Ghost Protocol, Rogue Nation, and Fallout (Fallout is not just my favorite of the series, I think it's one of the best action movies ever made)

School of Rock (2003)

Dumb & Dumber (1994)

True Lies (1994)

The Rock (1996)


10. Blues Brothers 

## 5. I'm looking for fantastical sword and sorcery movies with 80s adventure vibes, a unique atmosphere, wild quests, weird locations/monsters, and creative battles.

**Score:** 15
**Body:** So I've been getting really into sword and sorcery lately, because usually they're atmospheric, unique, creative, and just fun to watch and have a vibe most other normal action movies just don't. For example my favorites are the 80s Conan the barbarian movies, krull, willow, the sword and the sorcerer, the beastmaster, and for recent ones I absolutely love deathstalker 2025 and dungeons and dragons honor among thieves. I'm thinking of mentioning Excalibur 1981 and legend 1985 too, but I'm not fully sure if they're only  sword and sorcery movies. I'm asking because it's hard to search for these movies with the vibes I particularly want. I have conquest 1983 and Solomon Kane next on my watchlist. Have you guys got any other good ones I might have missed or that are underrated?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dragonslayer (1981)

Fire and Ice (1983)
2. Childhood favorite:

Kull the Conqueror (1997)
3. Ladyhawke
4. The Dark Crystal from Jim Henson and Frank Oz.
5. Clash of the Titans. 1981 version.
6. I’m going to watch Willow tonight just because of this post. 
7. The Labyrinth 
8. Red Sonja (1985)
9. Spine of Night
10. The Beastmaster

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Who is the best actress who has never won an Oscar?

**Score:** 2725

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/gb45vg9pfxah1.jpeg?width=1600&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=0494a894f03f9a7fc3f42f44c7ebbe48d4dde2fa

Glenn Close

Eight nominations but no wins is crazy
  - She's getting a honorary Oscar next year along with Director Ridley Scott. 
    - https://preview.redd.it/pd0f16qdixah1.jpeg?width=750&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=3dffcd701ab2e060580536fae66e917c0ca718ec

  
Finally!
2. You nailed it OP

![gif](giphy|fBSuEbNuEVfUGI0R0K)
  - [removed]
3. Kirsten Dunst. She’s an amazing actress. Won best actress at Cannes but has never won an Oscar. 
4. ![gif](giphy|3oriOeYGl5MKFtb2FO)
5. Hi, Toni!
  - She was haunting in Hereditary, it's a fucking travesty that she wasn't *nominated*

$10M budget and her gut-wrenching wails, her dinner table dialogue - the whole cast was stellar but she was **phenomenal**

I've loved everything I've seen of hers, and she is so gorgeous
    - Also great in The Sixth Sense and Little Miss Sunshine

## 2. What's your favorite Zooey Deschanel role?

**Score:** 1022

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Almost Famous.
  - The back-and-forth between her and Francis McDormand was great. So well written and performed.
2. Elf 
  - Baby, it’s cold outside 🎶
3. The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy
4. Who's that girl?!
  - It’s Jess 
5. ![gif](giphy|26ybxdaXB8YULeuVW)
6. https://preview.redd.it/and7x2x4j3bh1.jpeg?width=1239&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=55b511b2512680617db2833117a0b3e2fe42c280

*This* is Zooey Deschanel without bangs — I don’t know *who* the girl is on the right in the white dress (in this case, the theme song is “It’s *not* Jess”).

I know Reddit and all of the Internet is trying very hard to convince me that it’s her, but bangs or no bangs aside — her nose and eyes are different, and at this point I do *not* concede that the woman on the right is indeed Zooey Deschanel.
7. I didn’t know she had a forehead

## 3. What's your favorite actor singing a song in a movie for you? Mine is Merlin (Mark Strong) singing country Road Kingsman the Golden Circle

**Score:** 353

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Matt Damon - Scotty doesnt know
2. https://i.redd.it/vpki5xqu41bh1.gif

Maneater piano solo in No Hard Feelings (2023). Andrew Feldman kills it
7. Jaws - Robert Shaw,  Roy Schieder and Richard Dreyfus "Show me the way to go home".

## 4. What’s your favorite End of the World movie?

**Score:** 356
**Body:** What’s your favorite movie that tackles the end of the world trope in such a terrifying way, it’s unforgettable?

For me it’s Melancholia (2011). This movie gave me existential dread like crazy!

The sequence with the hail gave me anxiety like no other and the last few minutes are some of the scariest I’ve ever seen.

What’s your favorite end of the world movie?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cabin in the Woods 
  - https://i.redd.it/wxjov6jsd3bh1.gif

I LOVE THIS MOVIE 
2. I actually liked ‘Seeking a Friend for the End of the World’ (2012)

Don’t Look Up (edit: 2021, the above year confused me) is also good
  - *Seeking a Friend at the End of the World* is so underrated. Granted, I haven’t seen it since it came out, but I still think about the final minutes of the film quite a lot.  I’ll need to give it a rewatch. 
3. Hitchhikers Guide to the Galaxy
  - https://i.redd.it/9s6grljwb3bh1.gif

Been meaning to rewatch this! Thanks for reminding me! 💯💯
4. The Road 
5. This Is the End
6. Children of Men
7. Melancholia 

The Fountain

Edir: I realized I didn't read the subtext of the post.  OP literally mentioned only Melancholia. Lol. I can be a lazy redditor.

## 5. Are You A Fan?

**Score:** 290

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes. When the title slowly appeared on screen at the end, along with the score, I had a warm and fuzzy feeling spread throughout my body that just felt right.

When I watched it a second time with a friend, we both looked at each other at the end like “Yep, that was good.”
  - Don't cry in front of the Mexicans.

https://preview.redd.it/c73vewwcy3bh1.jpeg?width=1280&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=be97a5b1ea32ac3dcd1e30bc9a33c1f3ffe117dc
2. Didn’t like it the first time, but after several rewatches, it might be Tarantino’s best movie.
  - Every Tarantino movie is better on repeat watches, IMO. You know the plot, so you can pay more attention to, and appreciate the dialog.
    - Love him or hate him the man can write some of the best and most natural dialouge.
3. I love this movie. Leo is fantastic. 
  - Leo should have won the Oscar for this.
  - All right, that's too hot. Anything we can do about that heat?

Rick, it's a flamethrower.

https://preview.redd.it/nnrwxxspx3bh1.jpeg?width=640&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=4d2d8c05f926b26caa4e416763bf40a3eff4c635
4. One of my all time favourite movies. I know that’s an unusual (unpopular) opinion, but I absolutely love this movie. One of my favourite Tarantino films. 
  - It’s absolutely my favorite Tarantino film. It’s immensely watchable.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Under the Skin (2013)

**Score:** 48
**Body:** So, I've watched "Under the Skin" (2013) with Scarlett Johansson again. I tried to google what other people think. Many reviews talk about how the alien is first predatory, then learns empathy, and tries to understand the people. It appears strange to me that I haven't seen another obvious reading yet.

I think the woman is in fact not an alien. She is a sex worker, and the biker is her pimp. If you look carefully, right from the start, she doesn't really appear predatory. She is flirtatious towards men, yes, but also shy and almost scared of other people, for example, when a group of girls pull her into a club. She only looks for single, lonely men to lure them into her environment, just for them to vanish.

Now, if you were a movie director and wanted to visualize a scene of dissociation during unwanted sex, how would you approach it? Maybe you would refrain from showing explicit sex scenes. In the movie, as soon as she lures the men in, she literally steps back, and everything but her drowns in an oozing black void, a blackout, a space of absolute nothingness.

Later, after trying to lure the disfigured man, she stares into the mirror for a long time, examining a body that feels entirely foreign. She runs away, and as opposed to her charming flirtations in the beginning, she barely speaks now and seems confused, and even scared at times. She attempts to reassociate with the world, tries to eat cake, literally asks for help on the bus, and tries to be intimate with the guy who saved her. These look like the clumsy attempts of a deeply traumatized person trying to remember how to feel human, and how to inhabit a body for pleasure rather than utility, someone who might see herself as so disconnected from the world and its ordinary people as if she were, in fact, an alien. Eventually she runs away again, and the world breaks her before she can manage it.

In the woods, a man tries to rape her. Again, how would a director depict dissociation during unwanted sex? The rape scene is never shown, instead, we see the assaulter ripping apart her human shell, revealing a featureless, black alien underneath, before he runs away, only to return, douse her with gasoline, and set her on fire.

In her final moments, after being raped in the woods, which the movie only suggests when the assault shatters her seemingly human disguise, she truly recognizes herself as an alien and fully dissociates. She literally pulls off her own face and looks at it, still blinking.

So, the true journey of empathy is one the watcher has to make, where in the end, after being raped and burnt alive, she dies not as an actual alien, but as a lonely, isolated being.

...

Yes, I recognize that the original book (which I haven't read) may very well be about an alien. And yes, even the movie's own synopsis may also talk about an alien.

Still, the movie has its story in its own right, which  at the very least plays with vagueness. There are no spaceships, no backstory, just abstract images and strange, otherworldly music. Except for the end, when she sheds her skin, all alien elements are rather abstract. And the synopsis might be a trick to hide what is otherwise obvious in plain sight.

And yes, there are many sci-fi stories that hold deeper meanings and metaphors. For instance, "Gravity" (2013) is more about the disorientation following the death of a loved one than survival in outer space. Nonetheless, this is not what I am trying to argue here. I'm trying to say that in this very particular case the abstract nature of the sci-fi elements allows for a different interpretation where we're not witnessing the downfall of a predatory alien, but of a human being. This is not a matter of narrative allegories or metaphors; rather, it is a literal depiction of the raw psychological reality from her own point of view.

Undeniably, the film is full of symbolic imagery that involve disconnection, dissociation, and abandonment. Most notably, the crying baby on the stone beach, left behind by its parents who drowned at sea. It also draws from seemingly hidden shots of ordinary people, as if we are watching the stupor of the crowd from the outside through a completely neutral lens. Both the sea and the crowd elicit a sense of indifference, and the watcher may already wonder if they are as much part of the stupor as the people who are shown to live their mundane lives so carelessly.

Even without the sci-fi elements, there is a cosmic horror in the movie: Being utterly alone and abandoned in this universe, being disconnected from it all, and drowning either in the noise of the sea or the noise of the mundane and ordinary.

And if we cannot muster any empathy, then we are in fact the ordinary crowd, the ones who are disconnected.

...

EDIT: I concede that this interpretation might actually not be obvious or even intentional. I've really enjoyed the discussion here with you and even the downvotes. Thanks, guys. :)

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Interesting take i guess, and you're describing the intention of the film IMO, albeit through a different lens, but I can't get around the fact that you're going out of your way to reason/make sense of her not being an alien. Calling her NOT being an alien "obvious" is quite a statement lol. I'd be much more interested in how the movie displays she IS NOT an alien despite showing/telling you that she is, instead of your interpretation as to why she is a sex worker

Overall i'd say it's much less about being predatory, and much more about where empathy begins
  - In addition to Glazer being pretty explicit in interviews about it being an alien, the whole "birth" scene at the beginning of the alien, the lights from the space ship dropping her off. The black goo, the humans turned into food, the lack of genitalia, her peeling her skin off, you know, naturally, just about everything that happens in the movie. 😅 People can posit a theory about any movie being an analogy for whatever else, but it's about as obvious as possible in showing that the character is an alien without holding your hand in doing so.
    - Well said. This film doesn't need to be an analogy. What's depicted has more than enough meaning and doesn't need a hidden subtext. Why is she successful in her hunt, how does she go about picking her prey, why does she look the way she does, wouldn't she be hotter if she was blonde, why did her predecessor get taken out of commission.
    - Exactly. and like i said, i think OP understands the intent of the film.

 I will say that if she's human it actually negates some of the deepest messaging of the film (and book) that emotions and sentience aren't merely human, and that true empathy is extending that out to others that are unlike yourself but still going through the same existence. 
2. I am not personally a fan of theories where vast elements of the film, even those our POV character is not present for, are dismissed as all in a person's head. I think it is far more reasonable to say that you can see connections to a sex workers experience in the experience of the alien in this film. Under the skin is a masterpiece, but it is a minimalist one that allows all kinds of themes and experiences to be connected to it. That includes themes of gender, sexuality, empathy, and even environmentalism (though that and farming animals is much more explicit in the novel).
3. Been awhile since I've seen this excellent film. 

She seems to me a living alien worker/bio weapon that is used to prey on marginal men. The aliens make her "hot" but a very specific type of hot different from what ScarJo typically looks like in order to catch these certain men who are on the outskirts of society for whatever reason. She then takes them to be processed into space caviar or whatever the fuck they making to eat them. The men will probably not be missed. Why is that. She evolves to have emotions about her prey but she also seems to be expendable herself as witnessed in the fate of her predecessor. 

When she makes herself vulnerable it puts her in danger because we are alien animals and have our own way of seeing things as alien to us as her leaving an infant to die. That's why the Disfigured man affects her differently. He's not like the other guys but she fails to see why cause she has no frame of reference. I feel it's a fairly straightforward film in a sense. 

When you take away the alien aspect it's the plausibility of everything else still remains. And that's what elevates this Sci Fi film to greatness. It holds a disturbing mirror to ourselves.
  - IIRC, Johansson actually drove around and picked up random men. Those scenes in the car were her acting as if she was picking up men with real men who thought they were being hit on and then got into the car with her.
4. An interesting way to interpret it! 

I enjoyed the book quite a bit, but it's VERY different. It's more of a dark comedy that performs the narrative miracle of getting the reader to root for one of the most unsympathetic character tropes - a human-eating space alien.
5. I love this concept. It makes her sudden lack of speech at the end make more sense. I read that people say it’s not accurate to the book, but movie makers do this all the time where they put their own spin on the film. Thank you for this take. 
6. I like your interpretation of the movie, it does have a lot of vagueness. 
I don't know that the life of a lonely sex worker is what it was "actually" being depicted, but it is a truth that I can recognize. 

I like your post I just wanted to interact with it. 😆

## 2. "Coffee is for Closers" (Glengarry Glen Ross 1992) is this the greatest monologue of all time?

**Score:** 40
**Body:** I rewatched the other day, I've loved this film for a long time, but it really hit me with this Alec Baldwin scene and made me stop. "Is this the greatest monologue I've ever seen in a film?"

This is like if writing were Special Effects. You have this muddle of guys, working schmoes, they're chitchatting going about their day. Between that and the hub bub of small talk you have the constant rain making everything kind of a droning muddle.

And Alec aka Blake, just CUT through all that. Suddenly his voice singulary commands attention. Youre sharp suddenly focused on the intent of what he is saying.

Every word that ensues begins to paint a picture of these mens future, Alec is the unrelently certain fate that will befall each of them if they don't change course, manifest in flesh. He is every fear theyve had every doubt in their mind they have horbored about themselves being spoken out loud.

After he is finished you hate Blake, he's disgusting, a soulless yuppee who has no compunction letting these boys know he's better than them. What's even uglier is clearly got their through family connection which these men don't have.

But what's so damning is Blake is so ugly but so right also and thats what really stings. He's right more or less about these men in many ways, they likely didnt challenge themselves and ended up here and now have to come face to face with it here with him.

The speech literally leaves me not only wanting to go punch Blake but also step up and go push myself further, out of anger and spite toward what he said. To show him and anyone else who doubted you.

I don't know I just am not sure I've seen a speech so clever and effective at getting you to feel so many emotions at once. Rage, anger, ineptitude, fear and so on.

What did you think?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What's even more amazing about his monologue is that it was added for the film adaptation of the original play (both by David Mamet). It really adds a layer to the degradation these desperate men endure to make a living.
  - Adds to the degradation, and also elevates the stakes.
2. That's such a classic scene! Alec Baldwin is one of those quick-witted guys who just exudes confidence, which is why he was perfect for Glengarry as well as 30 Rock,

I'm not even really a fan of the movie itself, and I know it's cliche, but I always get emotional during the whole "people will come, Ray" monologue in Field of Dreams.

That court scene where Al Pacino absolutely destroys everybody in Scent of a Woman has to be up there too. "YOU DON'T KNOW WHAT OUT OF ORDER IS! I'M TOO FUCKING OLD AND TOO FUCKING BLIND NOW, BUT IF I WERE FIVE YEARS YOUNGER I'D TAKE A **FLAMETHROWER TO THIS PLACE**."

The "Milkshake" scene in There Will Be Blood has to be up there too.

"The Golden Watch" scene in Pulp Fiction.

Shawshank has quite a few incredible monologues, but of course Morgan Freeman's at the end is the best.

Harry Dean Stanton's monologue in Paris, Texas where he is recounting his memories of his ex-wife while talking to her on the phone in the peep show club is so heartbreaking.

These are just off the top of my head.

Edit - how could I forget the "all men are created equal" scene in To Kill a Mockingbird. "The defendant is not guilty, but *somebody* in this court room is...to believe firmly in the integrity of our courts, that's no ideal to me. That is a living, working reality."
  - Kevin Costner’s closing arguments in JFK is just like 15 minutes of talking but I’m locked in the whole time. 
  - > "YOU DON'T KNOW WHAT OUT OF ORDER IS! 

If only Al Pacino had any experience in that area.
3. The ultimate misread is Bill Hader doing this in his acting class in Barry. He gives it as though it's a positive pep talk. He says "second place a set of steak knives" like it's a nice thing. And third place you're fired like he's apologizing.
  - Bill Hader’s bad acting as Barry is some incredible acting, if that makes any sense 
4. My favorite thing about this monologue is that it has vestiges of a more conventional motivational speech, complete with Norman Vincent Peale-esque affirmations and mnemonics. It isn't hard to picture [Don Beveridge](https://youtu.be/_igaLv7ro8o?si=owha-P4xR-KWVou5&t=1284) ranting about A.I.D.A. and making dad jokes about brass balls. But what Mamet does is weaponize the banality of that whole genre.

I also find it endlessly funny how Blake holds out the Mitch and Murray leads like some kind of sacred relic. I have trouble believing they're really any better than the old leads. But even if they are, it seems like such a ridiculous and self-defeating Catch-22 to deny your salesforce the information they can use to make a sale unless they've already made a sale. 
5. I showed this scene to my GF who is an actress and had never seen it.  

Im also partial to Sheldon Levine’s phone hustle in the film.  

We actually went to see GlenGarry Glen Ross on Broadway, with Bob Odenkirk, which was amazing.  

The experience inspired to write a shoot a short film with some actor friends.  Of course not at Mamet level, but at least we got it done.  

You can see our crime short here 

https://youtu.be/CQSpHUFvxNo?si=QWMe--gAPUdJ-c3d
  - Why didn't you show her the whole movie? 

## 3. The "STAAY" scream from Obsession

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I found this to be one of the scariest parts of the film, She's laying so close to Bear as he's trying to slowly slide out from her grasp, you can feel the claustrophobia, it's like you're right there with him, and then as he's trying to escape she yells out this haunting scream, the way her eyes don't even open is so unnatural and disturbing

And then I found that people online thought it was a hilarious moment to ease the tension, which had me baffled. like their brain registered funny instead of fucking terrifying. In an interview the director was also surprised at how many people laughed at that moment

This scene is a perfect illustration of that Jordan Peel quote. "difference between comedy and horror is the music" both genres rely on rhythm, subversion and timing, they both use building up to a jumpecare/punchline to engage the same areas of the brain 

And so the Stay scene walks right at the middle of those two concepts, sorta like clowns. Some found it hilarious while others like me thought it was deeply disturbing.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. For some it’s nervous laughter because they are scared. For me I’ve just watched a ton of horror, and when something unique, super cool, or super clever happens, I laugh because it’s…I don’t know, just a delightful experience.

Nicki, does your Dad really have cancer. 

No, no, no,no, no, NO. Don’t do that! I thought we were having a nice date!!

She handled the instant, hyperbolic escalation in that scene beautifully in her line delivery but more importantly her facial expressions. I laughed in the theater and I wasn’t the only one. Of course people could have been laughing because they found it uncomfortable. It registered as over the top funny to me. In any case, I like to feel goosebumps when I watch horror movies and having a bunch of knuckleheads harharhar’ing during horror peaks isn’t my preference.

## 4. Fight Club(1999) and Four Lions(2010): pre-9/11 and post-9/11 black comedies

**Score:** 3
**Body:** September 11th attack shocked the film industry as much as everything else. After immediate reaction of censoring sensitive images, films changed their tone drastically. The most famous case is probably Independence Day(1996) and War of the Worlds(2005). Former treated mass destruction with tounge in cheek, "We will survive" tone, while latter is more serious and "Humans are real animals" tone. 

**Fight Club**

I recently watched Fight Club and it was very pre-9/11 with some late 90s film tropes. The 'Narrator' is dissatisfied with his boring but stable life and rebels to conformist society (American Beauty, Office Space). He joins radical, sexy underground group (Matrix). Their terrorism is treated tongue in cheek way with very little human cost (True Lies). 

I won't summarize entire plot of Fight Club, but the 3rd act is centered around Project Mayhem. Fight Club turned from guys punching each other to full blown cult, as members shave their head, moves to old house, and work day and night for free. Their actions evolved from feeding pigeons diarrhea medicine to blowing away credit card companies to end credit society. 'Narrator' freaks out of Project Mayhem but the movie mostly sticks to tongue in cheek or even anti hero tone. Only one death is shown and even that is a member shot by police. Tyler Durdon, leader of the club, insist no one will die from bombing.

**And why it reminded me of** **Four Lions**

The whole third act reminded me of Four Lions(2010). A British film about unbelievably dumb jihadists from Northern England. It's 180 degrees different from Fight Club. For example unlike Fincher's smooth and precise camera work in Fight Club, Four Lions uses documentary style hand held camera popularized by The Office. 

But the biggest difference is how they portray terrorism. Fight Club is led by muscular Brad Pitt with sexy outfit, and he gives charismatic speech about consumerism and masculinity. Pitt's portrayal of Tyler Durden is so great it got infamous for getting uncritical worship from naive audiences. 

Jihadists in Four Lions wears unassuming cloth, lives in dull place, gives ridiculous speech or even rap. Their belief is mix of pop culture, vague anger to consumerism and very limited knowledge about Islam (one guy can't understand how praying to Mecca works) Those jihadists' plan is wearing bomb under silly costume and blow themselves in London Marathon, which goes horribly but hilariously wrong. 

**Differences and similarites**

The difference between two films came from the period they were made. Fight Club was made when terrorism wasn't top concern in people's mind, even though we experienced major terror attacks in 1995 Oklahoma City and 1993 WTC. Four Lions was made when terrorism was major threat, to a level we overestimated the danger so much we invaded Iraq. 

But they have similarites too. They precisely portrayed how cults control followers by humiliation and sense of belonging. They twist our expectation on terrorism. Their protagonist is soft speaking cult leader who lost control what they started. And this is my favorite similarity, they have gay characters who joins group to prove their masculinity. Narrater in Fight Club is famously queer coded. Barry in Four Lions, the only white member of the group, is closeted gay man who 'test' other jihadists by making them pee on his mouth.

My writing got too long as I write. I hope it's readable. If you haven't watch these movies, I highly recommemd watching them both, preferably as double feature. They make each other even funnier, as I can see Fight Club is what extremists imagine themselves, but they're actually just idiots in Four Lions.
